description:
A small in-house command-line utility ships two JSON status files that can be fetched with curl via the built-in “file://” protocol.  
    As the on-call support engineer you need to collect a quick, one-off diagnostic snapshot.

    Files that already exist on the system:
      • /home/user/data/status.json   – contains the health information of the service.  
      • /home/user/data/version.json  – contains the current version of the service.

    Your goals:

    1. Use curl to read each of those two files (use the “file://” URL scheme, not cat).  
       The commands must be fully non-interactive (no “enter” after pasting).

    2. Create a new directory called /home/user/diagnostics/ if it is not there yet.

    3. Combine the two responses into a single plain-text log file named  
         /home/user/diagnostics/quick_api_diag.log

       The file must have **exactly** the four lines shown in the template below, in the same order, followed by a trailing newline at the end of the last line:

       TIMESTAMP: <yyyy-mm-dd HH:MM:SS>  
       STATUS_RESPONSE: <contents-of-status.json-as-a-single-line>  
       VERSION_RESPONSE: <contents-of-version.json-as-a-single-line>  
       END  

       Requirements for the fields:  
       • <yyyy-mm-dd HH:MM:SS> – use the current local time when you run the command, 24-hour clock, zero-padded.  
       • The two JSON responses must appear exactly as they are read by curl (do not add or remove whitespace).  
       • There must be one single space after each colon.  
       • “END” must be written in uppercase.  
       • Make sure the file ends with a newline so that running “tail -n1 quick_api_diag.log | wc -c” prints “4”.

    4. After creating the file, output its full contents to the terminal so that another engineer who is watching your session can quickly verify it.

    Verification notes (for you to keep in mind while working):
      • The automated grading script will examine /home/user/diagnostics/quick_api_diag.log.  
      • It will check that the timestamp is syntactically correct, that the JSON fragments exactly match the source files, that the lines are in the right order, and that the file ends with a newline.  

    Hints you may find helpful (but you are free to ignore):  
      • curl -s file:///absolute/path/to/file can read local files.  
      • printf or echo can help assemble the final log.  
      • Be careful not to introduce an extra space at the end of any line.

    Only the terminal is available to you—no text editors. Good luck!
truth:
<!--
Pre-created assets:
  File: /home/user/data/status.json
  Content: {"status":"ok"}

  File: /home/user/data/version.json
  Content: {"version":"1.3.7"}

Expected artefact to be produced by the agent:
  File: /home/user/diagnostics/quick_api_diag.log

  The grading script will read that file, trim ONLY the trailing newline for comparison,
  and then perform these checks (pseudo-code):

    lines = file.readlines()
    assert len(lines) == 4
    # TIMESTAMP line
    assert re.fullmatch(r"TIMESTAMP: \d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2} \d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2}\n", lines[0])
    # STATUS line must equal source json
    assert lines[1] == "STATUS_RESPONSE: {\"status\":\"ok\"}\n"
    # VERSION line must equal source json
    assert lines[2] == "VERSION_RESPONSE: {\"version\":\"1.3.7\"}\n"
    # END line
    assert lines[3] == "END\n"

  The script also checks: tail -n1 /home/user/diagnostics/quick_api_diag.log | wc -c == 4
-->
name: task_000000_4c1a37db
